  • Hide All Code

EC0866: Wildfire-specific fine particulate matter and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy in the U.S. ECHO-wide cohort

Sensitivity Analysis: Restricted to only include pregnancies where P=0 (first pregnancy)

Daniel Beene

March 30, 2026

1 Models

Models performed on censored dataset to only include primiparous pregnancies; n = 8073

1.1 Primary Adjusted

From AP: “Primary models will be adjusted for variables widely available across cohorts including maternal age, education, parity, race/ethnicity.”

  • Model: Generalized linear mixed model (GLMM) via glmer() from lme4
  • Outcome: Gestational hypertension (binary), modeled with a binomial family and logit link. Results are interpreted as odds ratios
  • Exposure: Each of 12 wildfire smoke metrics are modeled separately
  • Random effect: Random intercept for cohort (CohortID_UH3), accounting for clustering of participants within study cohorts
  • Covariates: Maternal age (splined), birthing parent educational attainment, parity, race/ethnicity, infant sex
  • Missing data: Models fit across 10 multiply imputed datasets via mice::with() and pooled using Rubin’s rules via mice::pool() Optimization: bobyqa optimizer with increased max iterations (maxfun = 2e5) and nAGQ = 0 for computational stability

1.1.1 Plot

1.2 Fully Adjusted

From AP: “Extended models will be additionally adjusted for covariates selected based on availability of harmonized variables across cohorts, including household size-adjusted income (or neighborhood deprivation index), maternal pre-pregnancy BMI, history of chronic hypertension, history of pre-eclampsia, pre-pregnancy diabetes, prenatal smoking.”

  • Model: Same parameters as primary adjusted model for exposure, outcome, cohort random intercept, handling missing data, and model optimization.
  • Covariates: Maternal age (splined), secular trend (year splines), census macro region, tobacco use, alcohol consumption, tract-level poverty, pre-pregnancy BMI, birthing parent educational attainment, parity, race/ethnicity, infant sex
